epigraph.text: I have told you these things so that in me you may have peace. In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world.

content.text:
Isosceles triangles have two congruent sides, and this symmetry produces 
special properties. Equilateral triangles, with all three sides congruent, 
exhibit even greater regularity.

content.title: Isosceles Triangle Parts
content.content:
In an isosceles triangle:

- The two congruent sides are called **legs**.

- The third side is called the **base**.

- The angle between the legs is the **vertex angle**.

- The angles adjacent to the base are the **base angles**.

content.title: Base Angles Theorem (Isosceles Triangle Theorem)
content.content:
If two sides of a triangle are congruent, then the angles opposite those 
sides are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\triangle ABC$ have $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BC}$.

Draw the angle bisector from $C$ to point $D$ on $\overline{AB}$.

In $\triangle ACD$ and $\triangle BCD$:

- $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BC}$ (given)

- $\angle ACD \cong \angle BCD$ ($\overline{CD}$ bisects $\angle C$)

- $\overline{CD} \cong \overline{CD}$ (reflexive)

By SAS, $\triangle ACD \cong \triangle BCD$.

By CPCTC, $\angle A \cong \angle B$.

content.title: Converse of Base Angles Theorem
content.content:
If two angles of a triangle are congruent, then the sides opposite those 
angles are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Equilateral Triangle Theorem
content.content: A triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ If $\triangle ABC$ is equilateral, then $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{BC} \cong \overline{CA}$.

By the Base Angles Theorem applied twice:
$\angle A \cong \angle B$ (opposite congruent sides $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{AC}$)
$\angle B \cong \angle C$ (opposite congruent sides $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{AB}$)

By transitivity, $\angle A \cong \angle B \cong \angle C$, so the triangle 
is equiangular.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Similar reasoning using the converse.

content.title: Corollary
content.content: Each angle of an equilateral triangle measures $60°$.
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text:
The sum of your word is truth, and every 
one of your righteous rules endures forever.
